title: Ramanujan complexes and golden gates in PU(3).
speaker: Shai Evra
abstract: In their seminal works from the 80's, Lubotzky, Phillips and Sarnak proved the following two results: (i) An explicit construction of Ramanujan regular graphs. (ii) An explicit method of placing points on the sphere uniformly equidistributed. These two seemingly unrelated problems, were solved by applying deep number theoretic Theorems (Deligne proof of the Ramanujan conjecture, Jacobi four square Theorem) on a single group form of PGL_2 over the field of rationals.

proof_stages.label: Ramanujan graphs via universal-cover spectrum
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the general definition of a Ramanujan graph as one whose nontrivial adjacency spectrum is contained in the spectrum of its universal cover, with Perron-Frobenius eigenvalues removed as trivial.

proof_stages.label: LPS explicit Cayley Ramanujan graphs
proof_stages.summary: The Lubotzky-Phillips-Sarnak construction is reviewed: matrices over the Gaussian integers satisfying a four-square norm equation give an explicit p+1 element generating set whose Cayley graphs over PGL(2,F_q) are Ramanujan.

proof_stages.label: Higher-dimensional Ramanujan complexes
proof_stages.summary: To generalize from graphs to complexes, the universal cover is taken to be an affine building and the Ramanujan condition is formulated using the relevant Iwahori-Hecke adjacency operators, while noting that known constructions mostly live in positive characteristic.

proof_stages.label: Optimal generators and golden gates
proof_stages.summary: The second LPS theme is recast for compact Lie groups: finite topological generating sets are called almost optimal when words nearly cover the group at essentially random-set density, and golden gates add a polynomial-time approximation algorithm after weakening exact shortest-word recovery by a bounded length factor.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic golden-pair framework
proof_stages.summary: The two targets are unified through a p-arithmetic subgroup lambda of an algebraic group over Q, where a golden pair requires tempered spherical representations in L2(lambda_q\G_p) and a simply transitive action on special vertices of the p-adic building, which then yields golden gates and explicit Ramanujan complexes.

proof_stages.label: LPS as the prototype golden pair
proof_stages.summary: The classical LPS case is identified as PGU(2) or Hamilton quaternions modulo center, where Deligne plus Jacquet-Langlands gives the Ramanujan condition and Jacobi's four-square theorem gives simple transitivity.

proof_stages.label: Obstructions to higher-dimensional generalization
proof_stages.summary: Attempts to pass to higher rank split into two failures: division-algebra forms have the desired Ramanujan property but fail simple transitivity by mass-formula growth, while matrix-algebra forms can have transitivity but violate the naive Ramanujan condition.

proof_stages.label: PGU(3) congruence-level remedy
proof_stages.summary: The main joint result is stated for PGU(3): a specific congruence condition at the prime above 2 defines a subgroup forming a golden pair for every odd q, because level restrictions exclude the bad representations and a finite calculation proves the group and transitivity properties.

examples.name: Function-field class-number-one contrast
examples.note: A function-field comparison illustrates that simply transitive arithmetic subgroups can exist in all dimensions in positive characteristic, unlike the characteristic-zero situation under discussion.
examples.engagement_reason: the function-field class-number-one phenomenon is invoked as a contrast with characteristic zero, without constructing or checking a specific case
